goal_spec_json.goal_statement: Sleep a minimum of 6 hours consistently every night

full_morning_briefing.definition: Evaluate the morning_message together with the same-day generated_actions, because both are shown to the user as one morning briefing experience.
full_morning_briefing.morning_message:
Hey Ameena, good morning.

You said you're feeling a 78 with high energy this morning — that's a solid start, and I'm glad you're feeling that way. Let's look at what your body's telling us. **Last night you slept only 5.85 hours** and didn't fall asleep until after 5 AM — that's almost 2 hours less than your usual and the latest onset we've seen. Your recovery score was 41, which is low, and your HRV dipped to 81 ms, both showing your system didn't get much deep rest. That tracks with yesterday's stress, which hit 59 and is climbing fast, and your mood and energy scores were down at 37 each. What's interesting is this is the third day you've reported feeling better than your physiology suggests — your body is running on fumes even though your mind says 'go.' The late sleep paired with almost no movement yesterday (zero exercise minutes and high sitting time) creates a cycle where low activity doesn't burn off stress, which then keeps sleep later.

Today is about breaking that cycle. The plan is to anchor your evening earlier, catch stress spikes in real time, and inject tiny movement breaks to keep energy from stalling.

Given how hard your body worked yesterday, protecting tonight's sleep window is your top priority to reset this pattern.

generated_actions.title: Set a 'last screen' alarm for 11:00 PM tonight and keep your phone outside the bedroom
fallbacks: If 11 PM is too early, set it for 11:30 PM but keep the phone out of the bedroom.
generated_actions.rationale: Sleep onset is critically late (5:13 AM yesterday) and trending sharply later (+56 slope_fast), while duration is short (5.85h) and declining. This strict, time-anchored screen boundary directly targets the 'very late sleep onset' identified in the holistic report, addresses the 'sleep_debt' risk flag, and supports today's priority to 'protect sleep opportunity'. It varies from recent suggestions focusing on pre-sleep breathing or body scans.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you set the 11 PM alarm and leave your phone outside the bedroom?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports setting and honoring the screen boundary, aiming for a sleep onset earlier than 3 AM (180 minutes) to reverse the sharply late trend.

assumptions: You have a smartphone with an alarm
You can charge your phone outside your bedroom
generated_actions.description: Set an alarm for 11:00 PM. When it sounds, put all screens away (phone, laptop) and charge your phone in another room for the night.

must_avoid: bringing screens back into the bedroom after 11 PM

generated_actions.title: When you feel tension building this afternoon, do 1 minute of box breathing at your desk
fallbacks: If you forget in the moment, do it at your next break.
generated_actions.rationale: Stress is elevated (59) and sharply increasing (+7.07 slope_fast), with reported emotions of 'Tense' and 'Sad'. This implementation intention provides a concrete, immediate coping tool to interrupt the stress spiral during your 'high workload' day, leveraging your adequate coping capacity (67) to build resilience against volatility.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you use the 1-minute box breathing reset when you felt tension this afternoon?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports recognizing a stress/tension cue and intentionally pausing to complete the breathing exercise.

assumptions: You can pause work for one minute
You can breathe safely
generated_actions.description: If you notice your shoulders tightening or your breath getting shallow while working, pause immediately. Inhale for 4 seconds, hold for 4, exhale for 4, hold for 4. Repeat for 1 minute.

generated_actions.title: After each hour of sitting, stand up and march in place for 60 seconds
fallbacks: If an hourly timer is disruptive, aim for 3-4 marches total today.
generated_actions.rationale: Activity status is 'critical' with zero MVPA yesterday, extremely high sedentary time (881 min) trending upward sharply (+73 slope_fast), and low steps (1842). This tiny, frequent movement directly counters the sedentary spiral, supports physiological recovery, and addresses the 'low_recovery' and 'volatility' risk flags within your 'tight schedule' constraint. It's a different approach from recent shoulder-roll or window-break suggestions.

evaluation.success_definition: Today's sedentary minutes are 840 or less (improving from yesterday's 881), indicating successful interruption of prolonged sitting.

assumptions: You can stand up safely
You have a timer
generated_actions.description: Set a recurring hourly timer on your phone or computer. When it chimes, stand up and march gently in place (knees lifting slightly) for 60 seconds before resuming work.

generated_actions.title: During your suhoor meal prep, prepare a calming herbal tea to sip 30 minutes before your target bedtime
fallbacks: If no herbal tea, drink a small cup of warm water with lemon.
generated_actions.rationale: Sleep schedule is severely drifted with very late onset. Habit-stacking a calming, non-caffeinated ritual onto your existing suhoor routine creates a concrete, sensory wind-down cue for tonight. This leverages your Ramadan meal pattern to build consistency, directly addressing the 'prioritize wind-down routine before late bedtime' soft constraint and the 'sleep' goal domain. It's a novel suggestion compared to recent screen-boundary or breathing actions.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you prepare the herbal tea at suhoor and drink it 30 minutes before bed?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports habit-stacking tea preparation to suhoor and using the warm, caffeine-free drink as a wind-down signal.

assumptions: You have access to caffeine-free herbal tea
You can safely consume warm liquids before bed
generated_actions.description: While preparing your pre-dawn suhoor meal, also set aside a caffeine-free herbal tea bag (like chamomile or mint). Place it with a mug near your bed. 30 minutes before you plan to sleep tonight, prepare and sip it slowly in low light.

You said you're feeling a 78 with high energy this morning — that's a solid start, and I'm glad you're feeling that way. Given how hard your body worked yesterday, protecting tonight's sleep window is your top priority to reset this pattern.

assumptions: User is observing Ramadan (meal descriptions indicate suhoor/iftar)
Sleep duration below 6h may be due to fasting schedule
Check-in scores (mood 37, energy 37) reflect subjective state despite high overall score
Journal missingness (confidence 0) limits insight into stressors and coping
Nutrition logging confidence (0.74) suggests partial but incomplete tracking
soft_constraints: maintain light activity to support recovery
be cautious with activity due to low MVPA trend
prioritize wind-down routine before late bedtime

previous_day_generated_actions.title: When you get into bed, do a 5-minute 'body scan' relaxation before reaching for your phone
fallbacks: If 5 minutes feels long, start with a 3-minute version focusing just on legs and torso.
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's sleep was fragmented with low efficiency (76.9%) and high wake ratio (23.1%), both trending worse. This direct, pre-sleep relaxation technique targets physiological calm to reduce sleep-onset latency and fragmentation, addressing the core sleep quality issue while respecting your high subjective energy (100) and low stress (12).

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you complete a 5-minute body scan relaxation before using your phone in bed?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports performing the focused body scan exercise prior to any screen use as part of their wind-down.

assumptions: You can lie down safely for 5 minutes
You have a pre-sleep routine
previous_day_generated_actions.description: As soon as you lie down tonight, close your eyes and slowly bring your attention to each part of your body from toes to head, noticing any tension and consciously relaxing it. Complete this before touching your phone or any screen.

must_avoid: using screens before completing the exercise

previous_day_generated_actions.title: After your first work session, stand up and do 2 minutes of light neck and shoulder mobility
fallbacks: If you forget, do it after your next break or meal.
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Sedentary time remains high (699 min yesterday) and is trending upward, contributing to the 'volatility' and 'low_recovery' risk flags amid your 'tight schedule' and 'high workload'. This tiny, habit-stacked action disrupts prolonged sitting, promotes bodily awareness, and supports stability without demanding significant time or energy.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you take a 2-minute mobility break after your first work session?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports intentionally pausing after work to stand and perform the gentle neck/shoulder exercises.

assumptions: You will have a work session
You can safely perform gentle neck/shoulder movements
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When you finish your initial focused work block today, immediately stand up and perform gentle neck rolls (5 each direction) and shoulder shrugs (10 slow reps) to release tension and reset posture.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Take a 12-minute walk after your iftar meal
fallbacks: If you can't go outside, walk in place or do 8 minutes of light stretching instead.
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Step count remains low (3608) and is trending downward, while physiological recovery is only moderate (proxy 56) despite excellent HRV. This gentle, post-meal walk directly counters sedentariness, aids digestion, and promotes blood flow for recovery without overexertion—aligning with the 'maintain light activity to support recovery' soft constraint and your high subjective energy.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you take a 12-minute walk after your iftar meal?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports completing the post-meal walk, and today's step count reaches at least 4500 (improving on yesterday's 3608).

assumptions: You are observing Ramadan and will have an iftar meal
You are physically able to walk for 12 minutes
You have access to a safe walking path
previous_day_generated_actions.description: After you finish eating your evening iftar tonight, put on comfortable shoes and take a slow, 12-minute walk outside or around your building. Focus on gentle movement and enjoying the evening air.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Set a 'last screen' alarm for 60 minutes before your target bedtime
fallbacks: If 60 minutes feels too long, start with a 45-minute boundary.
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Sleep efficiency is below average (76.9%) and declining, with a high wake ratio (23.1%). Your Ramadan pattern includes late meals; creating a clear, timed screen boundary before bed reduces blue light exposure and mental stimulation, directly targeting sleep fragmentation and supporting your 'consolidate_gains_and_optimize_sleep_schedule' priority and 'avoid sleep debt accumulation' hard constraint.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you set and honor the 60-minute screen-free alarm before bed?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports setting the alarm and avoiding screens for at least 45 minutes before bed, aiming for a sleep onset before 2 AM (120 minutes after midnight) to improve schedule consistency.

assumptions: You have a smartphone or timer
You have a target bedtime in mind
You have control over your evening environment
previous_day_generated_actions.description: Tonight, set an alarm on your phone for 60 minutes before the time you plan to sleep. When it sounds, put away all screens (phone, laptop, TV) and switch to a low-light, non-digital activity like reading, light stretching, or listening to calm music.

previous_day_briefing.morning_message:
Good morning Ameena! You're starting today feeling fantastic — a 92 out of 100 is a powerful place to begin.

Last night you got 7.3 hours of sleep, which is solid on duration. But the quality wasn't as clean as usual — your sleep efficiency was only 76.9%, meaning you spent about 23% of that time awake or restless. That's been trending down for a few days, which explains why you might not feel as restored as your high energy suggests. What's fascinating is that your body's recovery signal, HRV, is actually excellent at 123.5 and climbing — your nervous system is resilient even when sleep gets choppy. Yesterday, your energy and focus were both maxed out at 100 with stress at a super-low 12, yet your step count was only 3,608 and you spent nearly 12 hours sedentary. This gap between how you feel and how much you're moving is the story right now: you have incredible mental and emotional fuel, but your body isn't getting the same signal.

Today's about channeling that high energy into small, deliberate movements that support your system.

You've got the vitality — let's match it with a bit of gentle motion.

evidence_quotes: We truly live in a world led by crazy people
I hope everything is resolved soon enough

meal_descriptions.description: One fried egg, two table spoons of fava beans, and two tablespoons of feta cheese mixed with tomatoes and olive oil with an Arabic bread loaf
2 pieces of chicken panne, one cheese sambosak and two large spoons of pasta with minced meat and bechamel sauce cooked in the oven
Instant coffee and 2 pieces of sugar covered Egyptian eid sweets
